title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text: 2 What Is a Time Series?
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
2.1 Data with a Memory
Here is a question that seems almost too simple to be worth asking: what makes time series data different from any other kind of data?
The answer is not the subject matter. It is not that time series involve economics, or finance, or any particular domain. The answer is that time series data have a memory. Each observation is connected to the ones that came before it, and that connection is not a statistical nuisance to be corrected — it is the signal we are trying to model. Strip away the temporal ordering, and you have destroyed the most important thing the data contain.
This is what separates time series analysis from the rest of econometrics. In a standard regression course, the ideal dataset is one where every observation is an independent draw from some population — where knowing one row of the spreadsheet tells you nothing about any other row. In time series analysis, the exact opposite is true. We want the rows to be connected. We are trying to measure, model, and exploit those connections to say something useful about observations we have not yet seen.
That shift in orientation — from independence as an assumption to dependence as the object of study — has consequences for every tool in the econometrician’s toolkit. Estimators change. Standard errors change. Tests change. Even the questions we ask change. Understanding why requires starting from first principles, and first principles require getting clear on what kind of data we are actually working with.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 2.3 A Gallery of Economic and Financial Time Series
text:
2.3 A Gallery of Economic and Financial Time Series
Before formalizing any of these ideas, it is worth spending a moment with the kinds of data this course is actually about. Each series below illustrates a different feature that will become analytically important as the course develops.
Real Gross Domestic Product (quarterly). GDP measures the total value of goods and services produced in an economy over a quarter. US real GDP has grown persistently over the postwar period, punctuated by recessions — in 1980–82, 1990–91, 2001, 2008–09, and most dramatically in early 2020. The level has no fixed mean and no fixed variance; it trends upward through time. We will typically work with the growth rate — the percentage change from one quarter to the next — which fluctuates around a stable, if modest, positive mean.
Consumer Price Index (monthly). The CPI tracks the cost of a fixed basket of goods and services. Like GDP, its level grows almost continuously and has no stable mean. But the rate of change — inflation — behaves very differently across periods. The 1970s saw high, volatile inflation. The 1990s and 2000s brought a long era of low, stable inflation. The years 2021–2023 brought the sharpest inflation since the 1970s. Whether the inflation rate itself is stationary is one of the most actively debated empirical questions in macroeconometrics, and we return to it later in this chapter.
Equity index returns (daily). The daily percentage return on a broad stock market index fluctuates around a near-zero mean with no obvious trend. But large movements tend to be followed by more large movements — in either direction — a phenomenon known as volatility clustering. This dependence in the second moment, but not the first, is the hallmark of ARCH and GARCH models studied later in the course.
Nominal exchange rates (daily). The nominal exchange rate between two major currencies evolves as something close to a random walk: today’s rate is the best predictor of tomorrow’s, and changes are small and largely unpredictable. Exchange rates are a canonical example of a unit root process, and their near-unpredictability is closely connected to the efficient markets hypothesis.
Term structure of interest rates (monthly). Interest rates at different maturities move together but not in lockstep. The spread between long-term and short-term rates — the yield curve — has one of the strongest track records as a leading indicator of US recessions: when it inverts, recession tends to follow within twelve to eighteen months.
What these series share is that they are all ordered by time, all exhibit some form of temporal dependence, and all require tools designed specifically for that dependence. What distinguishes them — trends, cycles, volatility clustering, unit roots, seasonal patterns — is exactly what this course will teach us to characterize and model.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 2.3 Cross-Sectional vs. Time Series Data: A Conceptual Contrast
text:
2.3 Cross-Sectional vs. Time Series Data: A Conceptual Contrast
The distinction between cross-sectional and time series data runs deeper than it might first appear.
In cross-sectional analysis, the challenge is typically one of selection: are the units in our sample representative of the population? Are there confounders that make causal identification difficult? Statistical inference is built on the assumption that each observation is an independent draw from some population distribution.
In time series analysis, the challenge is primarily one of dependence: each observation is connected to the ones that came before and after it. This dependence is not a nuisance to be corrected — it is the signal we are trying to model. A GDP observation is interesting precisely because it is correlated with past GDP observations; that correlation is what makes forecasting possible at all.
This dependence has a profound implication for statistical inference. The standard formulas for standard errors, confidence intervals, and test statistics — derived under the assumption of independent observations — are generally wrong when applied to time series data. We will need new tools, and we will develop them carefully throughout this course.
There is also an asymmetry that has no analogue in cross-sectional work: we cannot re-run the past. With cross-sectional data, we could in principle collect a larger sample by surveying more households. With time series data, we have exactly one realization of history. The United States experienced exactly one 2008 financial crisis. Our entire statistical apparatus must be built on this single trajectory — a fact that demands both humility about what we can learn and precision about what we are assuming.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 3.1 The Question at the Heart of the Course
text:
3.1 The Question at the Heart of the Course
Forecasting means using what we know today to say something credible about tomorrow. It sounds simple, and in some settings it is. A meteorologist uses today’s atmospheric conditions to forecast tomorrow’s weather. A shipping company uses historical demand patterns to forecast container volumes next month. In economics and finance the same logic applies — but the execution is considerably harder, for reasons we examine shortly. Before getting there, it is worth being precise about what forecasting actually is, and distinguishing it carefully from two activities it is frequently confused with: cross-sectional prediction and causal inference.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
3.2 Forecasting vs. Prediction
In the machine learning literature, “prediction” refers to a cross-sectional exercise: using a set of observable features — income, age, education, location — to predict an outcome for a new individual. The time dimension is absent. The challenge is finding the right features and the right model, and performance is measured by how well the model generalizes to new units from the same population.
Forecasting is a fundamentally different exercise. We observe a sequence \(y_1, y_2, \ldots, y_T\) and want to say something about \(y_{T+1}, y_{T+2}, \ldots\) — values of the same variable, for the same unit, at future points in time. The conditioning set is a history, not a cross-section of features. The challenge is temporal extrapolation: using what the past behavior of a series tells us about its future behavior.
A model that fits historical data brilliantly may still fail as a forecast if it has captured features of the sample period that do not persist. This is the time-series version of overfitting, and it is one of the most persistent hazards in applied forecasting work.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
3.3 Forecasting vs. Causal Inference
Causal inference asks: if we intervene and change \(X\), what happens to \(Y\)? Answering this requires credible identification of a causal mechanism — a randomized experiment, a natural experiment, an instrumental variable — that separates the effect of \(X\) from confounders.
Forecasting asks only: given everything we observe up to time \(T\), what is our best guess of \(Y\) at time \(T + h\)? This is an exercise in conditional expectation, not causal identification. A variable can be a powerful predictor without causing \(Y\). And a genuine cause can be a poor predictor if it operates with long and uncertain lags, or if it is itself difficult to forecast.






WarningA Common Mistake



A finding that lagged values of \(X\) help forecast \(Y\) is tempting to interpret as evidence that \(X\) causes \(Y\). It is not. It says only that \(X\) contains information about the future path of \(Y\). Granger causality, which we study later in the course, formalizes this predictive notion precisely, and deliberately avoids claiming anything about true causation.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
3.4 Types of Forecasts
Point forecasts. A point forecast is a single number: our best guess of \(y_{T+h}\) given everything we know at time \(T\). If \(\mathcal{F}_T\) denotes the information set available at time \(T\), the optimal point forecast under squared error loss is the conditional expectation:
\[\hat{y}_{T+h|T} = \mathbb{E}[y_{T+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_T]\]
The quantity \(h\) is the forecast horizon. A one-step-ahead forecast has \(h = 1\); a four-quarter-ahead forecast for quarterly data has \(h = 4\). Point forecasts are the most common form in practice, but also the most incomplete — they say nothing about how confident we should be.
Interval forecasts. An interval forecast reports a range \([L_{T+h},\, U_{T+h}]\) within which the true value \(y_{T+h}\) is expected to fall with some specified probability — typically 80% or 95%. These prediction intervals describe uncertainty about a future realization, not about an estimated parameter. For a Gaussian process, a \((1-\alpha)\) prediction interval takes the form:
\[\hat{y}_{T+h|T} \;\pm\; z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \hat{\sigma}_{T+h|T}\]
Density forecasts. The most complete type of forecast is a full probability distribution over future values: \(\hat{p}(y_{T+h} \mid \mathcal{F}_T)\). A density forecast encodes the entire shape of our uncertainty — including skewness and the probability of extreme outcomes. It is particularly valuable in risk management and monetary policy, where the tails of the distribution matter as much as the center.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 3.5 The Loss Function: A Conceptual Introduction
text:
3.5 The Loss Function: A Conceptual Introduction
The optimal point forecast depends on the criterion we are optimizing. The loss function \(\mathcal{L}(e_{T+h})\), where \(e_{T+h} = y_{T+h} - \hat{y}_{T+h|T}\) is the forecast error, encodes our preferences about different types of mistakes.
Under squared error loss, \(\mathcal{L}(e) = e^2\), over- and under-forecasting are penalized symmetrically, and large errors are penalized much more than small ones — the optimal forecast is the conditional mean. Under absolute error loss, \(\mathcal{L}(e) = |e|\), the optimal forecast is the conditional median. Under asymmetric loss functions — when over-predicting inflation is costlier than under-predicting it, for instance — the optimal forecast shifts away from the conditional mean.
We return to loss functions in a dedicated chapter. The key point for now: the “best” forecast depends on the criterion we are optimizing. Specifying the loss function is not a technical afterthought; it is a substantive modeling choice.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 3.6 The Particular Challenges of Economic Forecasting
text:
3.6 The Particular Challenges of Economic Forecasting
Economic forecasting is harder than forecasting in the physical sciences, and it is worth being honest about why.
Structural change. Economic relationships evolve. The link between money growth and inflation that held in the 1970s broke down in the 1990s. Models estimated over one regime may perform poorly in another.
Measurement. Economic data are measured with error and revised substantially after initial release. GDP figures are revised multiple times over several years. A model that fits revised data well may have performed poorly in real time, when only the preliminary release was available.
The Lucas critique. Robert Lucas (1976) argued that the behavioral relationships in econometric models change when the policy environment changes. A forecast model that ignores this risks systematic error when policy shifts.
Reflexivity. Economic agents read and respond to forecasts. A forecast of high inflation can cause workers to demand higher wages, validating the forecast. This feedback between forecasts and outcomes has no real analogue in the natural sciences.
None of these challenges makes economic forecasting hopeless — but they do mean we should always be honest about what our models can and cannot do.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.1 The Time Index
Every time series analysis begins with a convention for labeling observations in time. The time index \(t\) runs over some ordered set \(\mathcal{T}\). In this course, \(\mathcal{T}\) is always discrete — a sequence of equally spaced points — so \(\mathcal{T} = \{1, 2, \ldots, T\}\) for a finite sample or \(\mathcal{T} = \mathbb{Z}\) (the integers) for the theoretical process underlying the data.
The time index is not just a label. Arithmetic on the index is meaningful: \(y_t - y_{t-1}\) is the change from one period to the next; \(y_{t-12}\) is the value exactly one year ago (for monthly data); \(y_{T+h}\) is the value \(h\) periods beyond the end of the sample. The information set \(\mathcal{F}_t\) denotes everything observable at time \(t\): the current value \(y_t\), all past values \(y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}, \ldots\), and possibly other relevant variables. The optimal forecast conditions on this entire history.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.2 Frequency
The frequency of a time series is how often observations are recorded. Common frequencies in economics and finance are:



Table 6.1: Common time series frequencies in economics and finance





Frequency
Observations per year
Typical use




Annual
1
Long-run growth, cross-country comparisons


Quarterly
4
GDP, national accounts, monetary policy


Monthly
12
CPI, unemployment, industrial production


Weekly
~52
Initial jobless claims, financial flows


Daily
~252 (trading days)
Asset prices, exchange rates


Intraday
Thousands
High-frequency trading, market microstructure






Frequency determines what patterns are visible in the data. Quarterly data cannot reveal within-quarter variation. Monthly data expose seasonal patterns — December retail sales, April tax payments — that annual data would average away.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.3 Regularity
A time series is regular if observations are equally spaced in time. Most macroeconomic data are regular: GDP is released every quarter, CPI every month. Regularity is what makes the arithmetic of the time index clean and the standard toolkit applicable.
Some financial series are irregular: individual transaction records arrive whenever a trade is executed. Irregular time series require specialized methods that we will not cover in this course.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
4.4 Notation
We will use the following notation consistently throughout the textbook.
A time series is denoted \(\{y_t\}_{t=1}^{T}\), or simply \(\{y_t\}\) when the index set is clear from context.
The lag operator \(L\) is defined by:
\[L y_t = y_{t-1}\]
Applying it \(k\) times gives \(L^k y_t = y_{t-k}\). The lag operator becomes an algebraic tool for expressing complex time series models compactly, as we will see in later chapters.
The first difference operator \(\Delta\) is defined by:
\[\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1} = (1 - L)y_t\]
The information set \(\mathcal{F}_t\) denotes everything observable up to and including time \(t\): \(\{y_t, y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}, \ldots\}\) and possibly other relevant variables.
We will frequently use the following Greek letters with specific meanings:

\(\mu = \mathbb{E}[y_t]\): the mean of the process
\(\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h})\): the autocovariance at lag \(h\)
\(\rho(h) = \text{Corr}(y_t, y_{t-h})\): the autocorrelation at lag \(h\)
\(\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(y_t)\): the variance of the process
\(\varepsilon_t\): an error or innovation term
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.1 Why Stationarity Matters
The mathematics of the difference equations section revealed something fundamental: the behavior of a time series process — whether its past influences decay, persist, or explode — depends on where the eigenvalues of its companion matrix sit relative to the unit circle. The ACF and PACF tools just introduced gave us a way to see this dependence structure empirically. The statistical concept of stationarity now translates both of these insights into the language of probability.
Stationarity is the condition that a process has a stable probabilistic structure over time. Not that it is constant — economic time series fluctuate continuously — but that the statistical properties governing those fluctuations do not themselves change. A stationary process today has, in a precise sense, the same distribution it had yesterday and will have tomorrow. This stability is what makes it possible to estimate parameters from a single observed history and trust that those parameters remain relevant for the future.
When stationarity fails, the usual machinery of statistical inference breaks down in several ways. Estimators may not converge to their population values. Standard errors may be wrong. Test statistics may follow non-standard distributions, rendering conventional inference invalid. Most practically, a model fitted to one part of the data may say nothing useful about another. The spurious regression problem — two completely unrelated nonstationary series appearing strongly correlated simply because both trend in the same direction — is perhaps the most well-known consequence, and it is one of the most dangerous traps in applied econometrics.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
5.2 Strict Stationarity






NoteDefinition 1.3 — Strict Stationarity



A stochastic process \(\{y_t\}\) is strictly stationary if, for any finite collection of time indices \(t_1 < t_2 < \cdots < t_k\) and any integer \(h\), the joint distribution of \((y_{t_1}, y_{t_2}, \ldots, y_{t_k})\) is identical to the joint distribution of \((y_{t_1+h}, y_{t_2+h}, \ldots, y_{t_k+h})\).


In plain language: shifting the entire process forward or backward by \(h\) periods does not change its distribution. The process looks the same statistically no matter where in time you observe it. Strict stationarity is a strong condition — it requires the entire joint distribution, not just moments, to be time-invariant.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
5.3 Weak (Covariance) Stationarity
For most purposes, we will work with a weaker notion of stationarity that restricts only the first two moments.






NoteDefinition 1.4 — Weak (Covariance) Stationarity



A stochastic process \(\{y_t\}\) is weakly stationary (or covariance stationary) if:

\(\mathbb{E}[y_t] = \mu\) for all \(t\) — the mean is constant over time.
\(\text{Var}(y_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty\) for all \(t\) — the variance is finite and constant over time.
\(\text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h}) = \gamma(h)\) for all \(t\) and all integers \(h\) — the covariance between any two observations depends only on the lag \(h\), not on when in time they occur.



Condition 1 rules out trending means. A series whose average grows over time — like the level of nominal GDP — violates this condition. So does a series whose mean shifts at some point.
Condition 2 rules out changing variance. A series that becomes progressively more or less volatile over time is not weakly stationary.
Condition 3 is the most subtle. It says that the dependence structure depends only on how far apart two observations are, not on where they sit in calendar time. The covariance between \(y_{2010}\) and \(y_{2011}\) should equal the covariance between \(y_{1990}\) and \(y_{1991}\). This is what makes the autocovariance function \(\gamma(h)\) a well-defined, estimable object.






TipWeak vs. Strict Stationarity



Strict stationarity with finite variance implies weak stationarity. The converse is false in general. However, for Gaussian processes — those whose joint distribution is multivariate normal — weak stationarity does imply strict stationarity, because the normal distribution is fully characterized by its first two moments.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 5.4 Stationarity and Inference: Why It Matters
text:
5.4 Stationarity and Inference: Why It Matters
Estimation. Under weak stationarity, the time average \(\bar{y} = T^{-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t\) converges to the population mean \(\mu\) as \(T \to \infty\) — the time series analogue of the law of large numbers. Without stationarity, this convergence breaks down.
Forecasting. Stationarity ensures that parameters estimated over the sample period remain informative about the future. A nonstationary process with a shifting mean, variance, or autocorrelation structure cannot be reliably extrapolated.
Spurious regression. If two nonstationary series both trend upward, a regression of one on the other will often yield a high \(R^2\) and a significant coefficient — even if the two series are completely unrelated. This is the spurious regression problem (Granger and Newbold, 1974), one of the most important pitfalls in applied time series econometrics.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
5.5 Transformations to Achieve Stationarity
Many economic series are not stationary in their raw form, but can be made (approximately) stationary through transformations. The two most common are:
Differencing. Taking first differences \(\Delta y_t = y_t - y_{t-1}\) removes a stochastic trend. If \(y_t\) is nonstationary but \(\Delta y_t\) is stationary, we say that \(y_t\) is integrated of order one, written \(y_t \sim I(1)\). GDP, price levels, and exchange rates typically fall into this category.
Logarithms. Taking the natural log of a series with an exponential trend linearizes that trend. For a level series \(Y_t\), we typically work with \(y_t = \log Y_t\), so that \(\Delta y_t \approx (Y_t - Y_{t-1})/Y_{t-1}\): the approximate percentage change. This is why GDP growth, inflation, and asset returns are almost always measured in log differences or percentage changes.
We will test formally for the presence of a unit root using the Augmented Dickey-Fuller test in Chapter 9.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.1 The Simplest Stationary Process
Before we can describe the full autocorrelation structure of interesting time series, we need a baseline: the simplest stationary process, one with no temporal dependence at all. This is white noise.
Think of white noise as the absence of pattern. It is a sequence of random variables that are mutually uncorrelated, have constant mean, and have constant variance. It is the residual that remains after all the predictable structure in a time series has been accounted for. In fact, one way to assess whether a forecasting model is well-specified is to check whether its residuals look like white noise — if they do not, predictable structure remains, and the model is leaving information on the table.






NoteDefinition 1.4 — White Noise



A process \(\{\varepsilon_t\}\) is a white noise process with mean zero and variance \(\sigma^2\) if:

\(\mathbb{E}[\varepsilon_t] = 0\) for all \(t\)
\(\text{Var}(\varepsilon_t) = \sigma^2 < \infty\) for all \(t\)
\(\text{Cov}(\varepsilon_t, \varepsilon_s) = 0\) for all \(t \neq s\)

We write \(\varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)\).


A white noise process is weakly stationary by construction: constant mean (zero), constant variance (\(\sigma^2\)), and zero autocovariance at all nonzero lags. Its autocovariance function is:
\[\gamma(h) = \begin{cases} \sigma^2 & h = 0 \\ 0 & h \neq 0 \end{cases}\]
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 6.2 IID Noise vs. Weak White Noise
text:
6.2 IID Noise vs. Weak White Noise
White noise as defined above imposes only zero correlation — it says nothing about independence. This distinction is important, and it is easy to miss.
IID noise is the strongest version: \(\varepsilon_t \overset{\text{iid}}{\sim} (0, \sigma^2)\). The observations are not just uncorrelated; they are independent and identically distributed. Independence is stronger than zero correlation: zero correlation rules out linear relationships only, while independence rules out any relationship of any kind.
Weak white noise requires only zero covariance. It is possible for \(\varepsilon_t\) and \(\varepsilon_{t-1}\) to be uncorrelated but not independent — knowing \(\varepsilon_{t-1}\) tells us nothing about the mean of \(\varepsilon_t\), but may tell us something about its variance. This is precisely the situation in ARCH models: the residuals are uncorrelated, but their squared values are correlated. Such residuals are weak white noise but not IID noise.






TipHierarchy of White Noise



From strongest to weakest:
\[\text{IID noise} \implies \text{Strong white noise} \implies \text{Weak white noise}\]
For this course, we will primarily work with weak white noise, noting when stronger assumptions are needed.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.3 Python Example: Simulating White Noise
The code below simulates three versions of white noise — IID Gaussian, IID Student-\(t\), and ARCH(1) — and plots them alongside their sample ACFs. We explain the ACF formally in the next section; for now, simply note that a white noise series should show no significant spikes in the ACF at lags \(h \geq 1\).


Show code — Figure 1.1
rng = np.random.default_rng(seed=42)
T   = 300

# IID Gaussian white noise
eps_gaussian = rng.normal(loc=0, scale=1, size=T)

# IID Student-t (df=3), rescaled to unit variance
eps_student  = rng.standard_t(df=3, size=T)
eps_student  = eps_student / eps_student.std()

# ARCH(1): sigma_t^2 = 0.2 + 0.75 * eps_{t-1}^2
alpha0, alpha1 = 0.2, 0.75
eps_arch       = np.zeros(T)
sigma2         = np.zeros(T)
sigma2[0]      = alpha0 / (1 - alpha1)
for t in range(1, T):
    sigma2[t]   = alpha0 + alpha1 * eps_arch[t - 1] ** 2
    eps_arch[t] = np.sqrt(sigma2[t]) * rng.normal()

series = [eps_gaussian, eps_student, eps_arch]
titles = ["IID Gaussian", "IID Student-$t$ (df = 3)", "ARCH(1)"]
colors = [EO_COPPER, EO_SKYBLUE, EO_SAGE]

fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 3, figsize=(12, 6))

for j, (y, title, color) in enumerate(zip(series, titles, colors)):
    # Time plot
    ax = axes[0, j]
    ax.plot(y, color=color, linewidth=0.8, alpha=0.85)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.6, linestyle="--")
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlabel("Time")
    ax.set_ylabel("Value")
    eo_style_ax(ax)

    # ACF
    ax = axes[1, j]
    plot_acf(y, lags=30, ax=ax, color=color,
             vlines_kwargs={"colors": color},
             title="", zero=False, alpha=0.05)
    ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.6)
    ax.set_title(f"ACF — {title}", fontsize=11)
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("ACF")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.9)

eo_suptitle(fig, "Three Flavors of White Noise")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 6.1: Three versions of white noise. Top row: time plots. Bottom row: sample ACFs. All three series have statistically zero autocorrelations at all lags. The ARCH(1) series shows visible volatility clustering in its time plot — stretches of large and small fluctuations — but this structure lives in the second moment and is invisible to the ACF.





Notice two things. First, all three ACF plots show no significant spikes at lags \(h \geq 1\): the autocorrelations are statistically indistinguishable from zero, confirming that all three are white noise. Second, the ARCH(1) time plot shows visible clustering — stretches of large fluctuations and stretches of smaller ones. This structure is real but lives in the second moment (variance), not the first, so it is invisible to the ACF.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.1 Measuring Temporal Dependence
Stationarity, as we will define it formally in the next section, is the condition that a process has a stable probabilistic structure over time. But before developing that formal definition, it is useful to have the tools to describe and measure what temporal dependence actually looks like in practice. Two processes can both be stationary while having very different patterns of dependence: one nearly uncorrelated from one period to the next, another remaining strongly correlated for many lags. The tools in this section let us characterize those patterns — and they will serve as our primary informal diagnostic for nonstationarity before we introduce formal tests.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
7.2 The Autocovariance Function






NoteDefinition 1.5 — Autocovariance Function (ACVF)



For a weakly stationary process \(\{y_t\}\) with mean \(\mu\), the autocovariance function (ACVF) at lag \(h\) is:
\[\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h}) = \mathbb{E}[(y_t - \mu)(y_{t-h} - \mu)]\]
By weak stationarity, this quantity depends only on the lag \(h\), not on the time index \(t\).


Several properties of the ACVF follow immediately from the definition.
Property 1: Symmetry. \(\gamma(h) = \gamma(-h)\) for all \(h\). The covariance between today and \(h\) periods ago equals the covariance between today and \(h\) periods hence. The ACVF is an even function.
Property 2: Maximum at zero. \(|\gamma(h)| \leq \gamma(0)\) for all \(h\). The variance of the process — \(\gamma(0) = \sigma^2\) — is always at least as large in absolute value as any autocovariance. This follows from the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality.
Property 3: Positive semidefiniteness. For any finite collection of lags and any real coefficients \(a_1, \ldots, a_k\):
\[\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{j=1}^{k} a_i a_j \gamma(t_i - t_j) \geq 0\]
This constrains the shape of the ACVF: not every function can be an autocovariance function.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
7.3 The Autocorrelation Function
The autocovariance has units — if \(y_t\) is measured in billions of dollars, \(\gamma(h)\) is in billions-of-dollars-squared — which makes it hard to compare across series or across lags. Normalizing by the variance gives a dimensionless quantity.






NoteDefinition 1.6 — Autocorrelation Function (ACF)



For a weakly stationary process \(\{y_t\}\), the autocorrelation function (ACF) at lag \(h\) is:
\[\rho(h) = \frac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)} = \frac{\text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h})}{\text{Var}(y_t)}\]
By construction, \(\rho(0) = 1\) and \(|\rho(h)| \leq 1\) for all \(h\).


The shape of the ACF reveals the temporal structure of the series:

Slowly decaying ACF: long memory or a trend; signature of nonstationarity.
Geometrically decaying ACF: autoregressive structure.
Sinusoidal ACF: periodic or seasonal component.
ACF cuts off sharply after lag \(q\): moving average process of order \(q\).
ACF indistinguishable from zero at all lags: white noise.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
7.4 Estimating the ACF from Data
We estimate the autocovariance from the observed series \(y_1, \ldots, y_T\) using the sample autocovariance:
\[\hat{\gamma}(h) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=h+1}^{T} (y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-h} - \bar{y})\]
where \(\bar{y} = T^{-1}\sum_{t=1}^{T} y_t\). Note the divisor is \(T\), not \(T-h\), even though only \(T-h\) terms appear in the sum. Using \(T\) ensures positive semidefiniteness of the sample ACVF matrix. The sample ACF is then \(\hat{\rho}(h) = \hat{\gamma}(h) / \hat{\gamma}(0)\).
Under the null hypothesis that \(\{y_t\}\) is white noise, the sample autocorrelations are approximately normally distributed with mean zero and variance \(1/T\) for large \(T\), giving the standard 95% confidence bands:
\[\pm \frac{1.96}{\sqrt{T}}\]






WarningA Caution on ACF Interpretation



The confidence bands assume the true process is white noise. When the ACF shows a clear pattern — slow decay, oscillation, seasonal spikes — interpret the shape as a whole rather than testing each lag individually.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 8.1 The Problem with the ACF Alone
text:
8.1 The Problem with the ACF Alone
The ACF conflates the direct effect of \(y_{t-h}\) on \(y_t\) with indirect effects transmitted through intermediate lags. Consider a series where \(y_t\) is directly influenced only by \(y_{t-1}\): \(y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\). Because \(y_{t-1}\) is correlated with \(y_{t-2}\), and \(y_{t-2}\) with \(y_{t-3}\), the ACF will show nonzero autocorrelations at all lags — not just lag 1.
The partial autocorrelation function (PACF) isolates the direct relationship. It asks: once we account for the intermediate observations \(y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-h+1}\), how much additional correlation remains between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-h}\)?
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 8.2 The Partial Autocorrelation via Partial Regression
text:
8.2 The Partial Autocorrelation via Partial Regression
The partial autocorrelation at lag \(h\), denoted \(\alpha(h)\), is the coefficient on \(y_{t-h}\) in the population OLS regression of \(y_t\) on its \(h\) most recent lags:
\[y_t = \phi_{h,1} y_{t-1} + \phi_{h,2} y_{t-2} + \cdots + \phi_{h,h} y_{t-h} + \varepsilon_t^{(h)}\]
The PACF at lag \(h\) is the last coefficient: \(\alpha(h) = \phi_{h,h}\). It measures the marginal contribution of \(y_{t-h}\) after all shorter lags have already been included.
The first two lags are instructive:
Lag 1. No intermediate observations to control for: \(\alpha(1) = \rho(1)\).
Lag 2. After removing the effect of \(y_{t-1}\) from both \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-2}\):
\[\alpha(2) = \frac{\rho(2) - \rho(1)^2}{1 - \rho(1)^2}\]
Lag \(h\). The general case is computed via the Yule-Walker equations:
\[\alpha(h) = \frac{\rho(h) - \sum_{j=1}^{h-1} \phi_{h-1,j}\,\rho(h-j)}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{h-1}\phi_{h-1,j}\,\rho(j)}\]
In practice, software handles this recursion automatically.






NoteDefinition 1.7 — Partial Autocorrelation Function (PACF)



The partial autocorrelation function (PACF) at lag \(h\) is the coefficient \(\alpha(h) = \phi_{h,h}\) on \(y_{t-h}\) in the population OLS regression of \(y_t\) on \((y_{t-1}, \ldots, y_{t-h})\). It measures the linear dependence between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-h}\) after removing the linear influence of all intermediate lags.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 8.3 Interpreting the PACF: The Diagnostic Table
text:
8.3 Interpreting the PACF: The Diagnostic Table



Table 8.1: ACF and PACF patterns for common time series processes










Process
ACF
PACF




AR(\(p\))
Decays to zero (geometrically or oscillating)
Cuts off sharply after lag \(p\)


MA(\(q\))
Cuts off sharply after lag \(q\)
Decays to zero


ARMA(\(p\),\(q\))
Decays to zero
Decays to zero


White noise
Zero at all lags
Zero at all lags


Random walk
Decays very slowly
Large spike only at lag 1






The AR(\(p\)) result follows directly from the partial regression interpretation: only the \(p\) most recent lags directly influence \(y_t\), so the PACF is exactly zero at all lags greater than \(p\).
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
7.1 The Unit Root Problem
The most important form of nonstationarity in economic time series is the unit root — a stochastic trend that causes the series to wander without bound. To understand what this means, start with the simplest case: the random walk.
\[y_t = y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t, \qquad \varepsilon_t \sim WN(0, \sigma^2)\]
Starting from \(y_0\), we have \(y_t = y_0 + \sum_{s=1}^{t} \varepsilon_s\). The variance grows without bound — \(\text{Var}(y_t) = t\sigma^2\) — so the process is clearly not stationary. The hallmark of a random walk is that shocks are permanent: a positive shock today shifts \(y_t\) up, and it never returns to its previous level. Economic examples include the level of nominal stock prices, nominal exchange rates, and price levels.
Now consider a slightly more general process:
\[y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\]
If \(|\phi| < 1\), this is a stationary process: shocks are transitory, decaying at rate \(|\phi|^h\) after \(h\) periods. If \(\phi = 1\), we have a random walk. Subtracting \(y_{t-1}\) from both sides and defining \(\delta = \phi - 1\):
\[\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\]
This simple rearrangement reveals the testing problem clearly. The unit root null is \(H_0: \delta = 0\) (equivalently \(\phi = 1\)). The stationary alternative is \(H_1: \delta < 0\) (equivalently \(\phi < 1\)). This is the regression we will estimate and test.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 9.2 From the Random Walk to the AR(1)
text:
9.2 From the Random Walk to the AR(1)
Generalizing to \(y_t = \phi y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\) and subtracting \(y_{t-1}\) from both sides with \(\delta = \phi - 1\):
\[\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1} + \varepsilon_t\]
The unit root null is \(H_0: \delta = 0\) (\(\phi = 1\)); the stationary alternative is \(H_1: \delta < 0\) (\(\phi < 1\)).
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.3 The Basic Dickey-Fuller Test
The Dickey-Fuller test estimates:
\[\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \delta y_{t-1} + u_t\]
and tests \(H_0: \delta = 0\) against \(H_1: \delta < 0\). Three specifications exist:



Table 9.1: Dickey-Fuller specification choices










Specification
Regression
Use when…




No constant, no trend
\(\Delta y_t = \delta y_{t-1} + u_t\)
Series has no drift, zero mean


Constant only
\(\Delta y_t = \alpha + \delta y_{t-1} + u_t\)
Series may have a non-zero mean


Constant and trend
\(\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \delta y_{t-1} + u_t\)
Series may have a linear trend under \(H_1\)






The critical values are not standard \(t\)-distribution values. Under \(H_0\), \(y_{t-1}\) is integrated, giving the OLS estimator \(\hat{\delta}\) a non-standard left-skewed asymptotic distribution — the Dickey-Fuller distribution. Using standard \(t\) critical values would severely under-reject the unit root null.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.4 The Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test
The basic DF test assumes \(u_t\) is white noise. The ADF test adds lagged differences to absorb serial correlation:
\[\Delta y_t = \alpha + \beta t + \delta y_{t-1} + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \psi_j \Delta y_{t-j} + u_t\]
The null remains \(H_0: \delta = 0\), and the test statistic is the \(t\)-ratio on \(\hat{\delta}\):
\[\text{ADF statistic} = \frac{\hat{\delta}}{\text{se}(\hat{\delta})}\]
The augmentation lags do not change the asymptotic distribution under \(H_0\).
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.5 Choosing the Lag Length \(p\)
Information criteria. Select \(p\) to minimize AIC or BIC:
\[\text{AIC}(p) = \log\hat{\sigma}^2_p + \frac{2(p+k)}{T} \qquad \text{BIC}(p) = \log\hat{\sigma}^2_p + \frac{\log(T)(p+k)}{T}\]
General-to-specific (GETS). Start with \(p_{\max} = \lfloor 12(T/100)^{1/4} \rfloor\) (Schwert rule). Drop the last lag if insignificant and repeat until a significant lag is found or \(p = 0\).
We use AIC-based selection throughout this textbook.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.6 Interpreting ADF Results
The output consists of: (1) the test statistic — more negative means stronger evidence against the unit root; (2) the p-value — below 0.05 leads to rejection; (3) critical values at 1%, 5%, and 10%.






WarningTwo Common Mistakes



Mistake 1: Failing to reject \(H_0\) is not evidence for a unit root. The ADF has low power against near-unit-root alternatives.
Mistake 2: The specification matters. Always inspect the time plot before choosing whether to include a constant and/or trend.


The complement of the ADF — where the null is stationarity — is the KPSS test. Running both and checking for consistency is good practice.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 10.1 Getting Our Hands on the Data
text:
10.1 Getting Our Hands on the Data
We now put all the tools developed in this chapter to work on real economic data. We use two series from FRED:

GDPC1: US Real GDP, chained 2017 dollars, quarterly, seasonally adjusted.
CPIAUCSL: US CPI for All Urban Consumers, monthly, seasonally adjusted.



Show code — Data download
print("Real GDP (last 5 observations):")
print(gdp[["Real GDP", "GDP Growth (QoQ)"]].tail().round(3).to_string())

      f"{gdp.index[0].date()} to {gdp.index[-1].date()}")

print(f"\n{cpi.shape[0]} monthly observations, "
      f"{cpi.index[0].date()} to {cpi.index[-1].date()}")

The summary statistics already tell an interesting story. title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.3 Time Plots
The first thing we always do with a new time series is plot it. A well-constructed time plot reveals trend, seasonality, structural breaks, outliers, and volatility clustering — features that inform every subsequent modeling decision.


Show code — Figure 1.2
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 7))

# GDP Level
ax = axes[0, 0]
ax.plot(gdp.index, gdp["Real GDP"], color=EO_COPPER, linewidth=1.5)
ax.set_title("US Real GDP — Level")
ax.set_ylabel("Billions of Chained 2017 $")
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(
    mticker.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f"${x/1000:.0f}T"))

# GDP Growth
ax = axes[0, 1]
gdp_growth = gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna()
ax.fill_between(gdp_growth.index, gdp_growth, 0,
                where=gdp_growth >= 0,
                color=EO_SAGE, alpha=0.7, label="Positive")
ax.fill_between(gdp_growth.index, gdp_growth, 0,
                where=gdp_growth < 0,
                color=EO_TERRACOTTA, alpha=0.7, label="Negative")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.7, linestyle="--")
ax.set_title("US Real GDP — QoQ Growth Rate")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

# CPI Level
ax = axes[1, 0]
ax.plot(cpi.index, cpi["CPI"], color=EO_SKYBLUE, linewidth=1.5)
ax.set_title("US CPI — Level")
ax.set_ylabel("Index (1982–84 = 100)")

# CPI Inflation
ax = axes[1, 1]
inf_series = cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna()
ax.plot(inf_series.index, inf_series, color=EO_SKYBLUE, linewidth=1.2)
ax.axhline(inf_series.mean(), color=EO_COPPER, linewidth=0.9,
           linestyle="--", label=f"Mean = {inf_series.mean():.1f}%")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.5, linestyle=":")
ax.set_title("US CPI — Year-on-Year Inflation")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(fontsize=8)

for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xlabel("")
    eo_style_ax(ax)

eo_suptitle(fig, "US Real GDP and CPI, 1960–2024")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 10.1: US Real GDP and CPI, 1960–2024. Left panels show levels; right panels show the QoQ growth rate (GDP) and year-on-year inflation (CPI). The level series are clearly nonstationary — both trend upward persistently. The transformed series are much closer to stationarity, though both show periods of higher and lower volatility. The COVID-19 contraction in Q2 2020 is the most extreme observation in the GDP growth series.





The level series in the left panels tell a clear story: both GDP and CPI trend persistently upward. No fixed mean exists; variances are not constant. These are nonstationary series. The transformed series in the right panels look much more like stationary fluctuations around a stable mean — though even here, the inflation series shows periods of higher and lower volatility.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.4 ACF and PACF Analysis
We now examine the autocorrelation structure of each series. The contrast between levels and transformed series is the clearest possible illustration of the theory developed in the previous sections.


Show code — Figure 1.3
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 7))

plot_acf(gdp["Log Real GDP"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[0, 0],
         color=EO_COPPER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_COPPER},
         title="ACF — Log Real GDP (Level)", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(gdp["Log Real GDP"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[0, 1],
          color=EO_COPPER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_COPPER},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Log Real GDP (Level)",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_acf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[1, 0],
         color=EO_SAGE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SAGE},
         title="ACF — GDP Growth Rate", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[1, 1],
          color=EO_SAGE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SAGE},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — GDP Growth Rate",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.9)

eo_suptitle(fig, "ACF and PACF: US Real GDP")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 10.2: ACF and PACF for US Real GDP. Top row: log level. Bottom row: QoQ growth rate. The log-level ACF decays extremely slowly — barely moving from 1.0 across many lags — the unmistakable signature of a nonstationary process. After differencing, the ACF of GDP growth drops sharply and the PACF shows one dominant spike at lag 1, suggesting a low-order AR model for growth rates.







Show code — Figure 1.4
fig, axes = plt.subplots(2, 2, figsize=(12, 7))

plot_acf(cpi["Log CPI"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[0, 0],
         color=EO_SKYBLUE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SKYBLUE},
         title="ACF — Log CPI (Level)", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(cpi["Log CPI"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[0, 1],
          color=EO_SKYBLUE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SKYBLUE},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Log CPI (Level)",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_acf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[1, 0],
         color=EO_LAVENDER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_LAVENDER},
         title="ACF — Year-on-Year Inflation", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[1, 1],
          color=EO_LAVENDER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_LAVENDER},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Year-on-Year Inflation",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

for ax in axes.flatten():
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.9)

eo_suptitle(fig, "ACF and PACF: US CPI")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 10.3: ACF and PACF for US CPI. Top row: log level. Bottom row: year-on-year inflation. The log-level CPI has a near-unit ACF — clearly nonstationary. The inflation series has a more complex autocorrelation structure: the ACF decays slowly and remains significant at many lags, while the PACF drops sharply after lag 2. This is consistent with a low-order AR or ARMA process for inflation.





The slowly decaying ACF of the log-level series is the visual fingerprint of a unit root — exactly what theory predicts for an \(I(1)\) process. After transformation, the ACF drops quickly, though for inflation significant autocorrelation persists at many lags, pointing to richer dynamics than simple white noise.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.5 Formal Stationarity Tests
Visual inspection and ACF plots give us strong intuitions. We now formalize them with the ADF test across all four series.


Show code — ADF tests
def run_adf(series, name, regression="ct"):
    """Run ADF test and print formatted results."""
    clean  = series.dropna()
    result = adfuller(clean, regression=regression, autolag="AIC")

    adf_stat  = result[0]
    p_value   = result[1]
    n_lags    = result[2]
    crit_vals = result[4]
    conclusion = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary" if p_value < 0.05
                  else "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root")

    print(f"\n{'─'*52}")
    print(f"  ADF Test: {name}")
    print(f"  Specification: '{regression}'")
    print(f"{'─'*52}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {adf_stat:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {p_value:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (AIC)       : {n_lags:>9d}")
    for level, cv in crit_vals.items():
        print(f"  Critical value {level:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {conclusion}")
    print(f"{'─'*52}")

run_adf(gdp["Log Real GDP"],      "Log Real GDP (Level)",   regression="ct")
run_adf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"],  "GDP QoQ Growth Rate",    regression="c")
run_adf(cpi["Log CPI"],           "Log CPI (Level)",        regression="ct")
run_adf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"],   "Year-on-Year Inflation", regression="c")

  Specification: 'ct'
────────────────────────────────────────────────────

  p-value          :    0.7180
  Lags (AIC)       :         0
  Critical value   1%:   -3.9944
  Critical value   5%:   -3.4277
  Critical value  10%:   -3.1372
  Conclusion       : Fail to reject H₀ → Unit root

────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ADF Test: GDP QoQ Growth Rate
  Specification: 'c'
────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Test statistic   :  -15.3642
  p-value          :    0.0000
  Lags (AIC)       :         0
  Critical value   1%:   -3.4562
  Critical value   5%:   -2.8729
  Critical value  10%:   -2.5728
  Conclusion       : Reject H₀ → Stationary
────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ADF Test: Log CPI (Level)
  Specification: 'ct'
────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Test statistic   :   -1.2986
  p-value          :    0.8883
  Lags (AIC)       :        15
  Critical value   1%:   -3.9708
  Critical value   5%:   -3.4163
  Critical value  10%:   -3.1305
  Conclusion       : Fail to reject H₀ → Unit root
────────────────────────────────────────────────────

────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  ADF Test: Year-on-Year Inflation
  Specification: 'c'
────────────────────────────────────────────────────
  Test statistic   :   -3.1989
  p-value          :    0.0200
  Lags (AIC)       :        15
  Critical value   1%:   -3.4391
  Critical value   5%:   -2.8654
  Critical value  10%:   -2.5688
  Conclusion       : Reject H₀ → Stationary
────────────────────────────────────────────────────


The results confirm what the time plots and ACF/PACF analysis suggested. Log Real GDP and Log CPI both fail to reject the unit root null — the test statistics are close to zero, far less negative than the critical values, and p-values are large. Both level series are nonstationary \(I(1)\) processes.
GDP Growth rejects the unit root decisively: the test statistic is strongly negative, well below the 1% critical value, with a p-value essentially zero. Year-on-Year Inflation rejects the unit root at the 5% level, though the literature on inflation persistence is extensive precisely because inflation can behave like a near-\(I(1)\) process over certain subperiods.






NoteSummary of Integration Orders



Log GDP and log CPI are \(I(1)\): nonstationary in levels, stationary in first differences. GDP growth and inflation are \(I(0)\): stationary. This is a classic and well-established finding in macroeconometrics that motivates essentially all the model specifications we develop in this course.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
10.6 Summary Statistics Table


Show code — Summary table
def summary_row(series, name, regression):
    clean  = series.dropna()
    result = adfuller(clean, regression=regression, autolag="AIC")
    stars  = ("***" if result[1] < 0.01 else
              "**"  if result[1] < 0.05 else
              "*"   if result[1] < 0.10 else "")
    return {
        "Series"     : name,
        "Obs"        : len(clean),
        "Mean"       : round(clean.mean(), 3),
        "Std. Dev."  : round(clean.std(),  3),
        "Min"        : round(clean.min(),  3),
        "Max"        : round(clean.max(),  3),
        "ADF Stat"   : f"{result[0]:.3f}{stars}",
        "p-value"    : round(result[1], 3),
        "Lags (AIC)" : result[2],
        "Spec."      : regression,
    }

rows = [
    summary_row(gdp["Log Real GDP"],      "Log Real GDP",        "ct"),
    summary_row(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"],  "GDP Growth (QoQ, %)", "c"),
    summary_row(cpi["Log CPI"],           "Log CPI",             "ct"),
    summary_row(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"],   "Inflation (YoY, %)","c"),
]

summary_df = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("Series")
print(summary_df.to_string())
print("\n*** p < 0.01  ** p < 0.05  * p < 0.10")


                     Obs   Mean  Std. Dev.    Min     Max    ADF Stat  p-value  Lags (AIC) Spec.
Series

GDP Growth (QoQ, %)  257  0.735      1.064 -8.204   7.476  -15.364***    0.000           0     c
Log CPI              774  4.702      0.747  3.380   5.747      -1.299    0.888          15    ct
Inflation (YoY, %)   762  3.697      2.669 -1.978  13.621    -3.199**    0.020          15     c
