crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.1 Starting with Visual Inspection
Before reaching for a formal statistical test, the first step in assessing stationarity is always visual inspection. A well-constructed time plot reveals most of what we need to know.
A trend-stationary series shows a clear upward or downward drift, but fluctuations around that drift appear roughly constant in amplitude. A straight line through the data looks like a reasonable fit.
A difference-stationary (unit root) series wanders without apparent direction. There is no deterministic path the data seem to track. The series drifts up for a while, then drifts back down, with no tendency to return to a fixed level. The variance appears to grow with time — consistent with what we showed analytically for the random walk.
An explosive series accelerates: the rate of change increases over time, and the series leaves any reference path behind at increasing speed.
Looking at the sample ACF provides a further informal diagnostic. A stationary series has an ACF that decays reasonably quickly toward zero. A unit root series has an ACF that decays extremely slowly — often remaining close to 1.0 for many lags — exactly as we saw in the figures above for log GDP and log CPI. These visual tools are informative but imprecise. For formal inference, we need the ADF and KPSS tests.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.1 Detrending
If a series is trend stationary, the right fix is to remove the deterministic trend. For a linear trend, regress \(y_t\) on a constant and time \(t\), and work with the residuals \(\hat{u}_t = y_t - \hat\alpha - \hat\beta t\).
Detrending is appropriate when shocks are transitory — the series wanders away from its trend for a while but returns. Applying detrending to a difference-stationary series produces spurious detrending: the “trend” estimated by OLS is itself a random variable, and the residuals are not stationary.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.2 First Differencing
If a series is difference stationary — has a unit root — take first differences. The differenced series \(\Delta y_t\) is \(I(0)\) by construction when \(y_t \sim I(1)\), and all standard methods apply to the differenced series.
Differencing does have a cost: it removes information about the long-run level. If two series share a common stochastic trend — they are cointegrated — differencing each one separately discards the long-run relationship between them. Cointegration is covered in a later chapter.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.3 Log Transformation and Log-Differencing
Many economic series — GDP, price levels, asset prices — grow exponentially. The natural logarithm linearizes this growth, converting multiplicative factors into additive increments. Beyond linearizing the trend, log transformation stabilizes variance: a series growing exponentially tends to have variance proportional to its level, and logs remove this level-dependence.
Combining log transformation with first differencing gives log-differencing:
\[\Delta y_t = \log Y_t - \log Y_{t-1} = \log\!\left(\frac{Y_t}{Y_{t-1}}\right) \approx \frac{Y_t - Y_{t-1}}{Y_{t-1}}\]
where the last approximation holds for small changes. Log-differencing gives the approximate percentage change — a natural, interpretable unit for growth rates, inflation, and returns.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.4 Seasonal Differencing
For series with strong seasonal patterns, the seasonal difference operator removes periodicity:
\[\Delta_s y_t = y_t - y_{t-s} = (1 - L^s)y_t\]
Seasonal differencing compares each observation to the same period a year earlier, removing the seasonal component directly.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.5 Choosing the Right Transformation
Three criteria should guide the choice:
Formal test results. Apply ADF and KPSS to both the level and transformed series. If both tests agree on a unit root for the level series but agree on stationarity for the differenced series, first differencing has successfully achieved stationarity.
Visual inspection. Plot both the original and transformed series. The transformed series should fluctuate around a stable mean with roughly constant variance.
Economic interpretability. Log-differenced GDP is the quarterly growth rate — a quantity with direct economic meaning. Where the transformation has a natural economic interpretation, it should be preferred over a purely mechanical one.


Show code — Figure 1.4
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(6, 9))

ax = axes[0]
ax.plot(gdp.index, gdp["Log Real GDP"], color=EO_COPPER, linewidth=1.2)
ax.set_title("Log Real GDP — Level")
ax.set_ylabel("Log billions (2017 $)")
eo_style_ax(ax)

ax = axes[1]
gdp_g = gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna()
ax.fill_between(gdp_g.index, gdp_g, 0,
                where=gdp_g >= 0, color=EO_SAGE, alpha=0.7, label="Positive")
ax.fill_between(gdp_g.index, gdp_g, 0,
                where=gdp_g < 0, color=EO_TERRACOTTA, alpha=0.7, label="Negative")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.6, linestyle="--")
ax.set_title("Real GDP — QoQ Growth Rate (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

ax = axes[2]
ax.plot(cpi.index, cpi["Log CPI"], color=EO_SKYBLUE, linewidth=1.2)
ax.set_title("Log CPI — Level")
ax.set_ylabel("Log index (1982–84 = 100)")
eo_style_ax(ax)

ax = axes[3]
inf = cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna()
ax.plot(inf.index, inf, color=EO_SKYBLUE, linewidth=1.0)
ax.axhline(inf.mean(), color=EO_COPPER, linewidth=0.8, linestyle="--",
           label=f"Mean = {inf.mean():.1f}%")
ax.axhline(0, color=EO_CHARCOAL, linewidth=0.4, linestyle=":")
ax.set_title("CPI — Year-on-Year Inflation (%)")
ax.set_ylabel("Percent")
ax.legend(fontsize=6)
eo_style_ax(ax)

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("")

eo_suptitle(fig, "US Real GDP and CPI: Levels and Transformations, 1960–2024")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 11.1: US real GDP and CPI: levels and stationary transformations. Left column: log levels, showing persistent upward trends and growing variance — the visual signature of integrated processes. Right column: log-differenced series — QoQ growth rate for GDP (top) and year-on-year inflation for CPI (bottom) — fluctuating around stable means. The COVID-19 contraction and recovery (2020) is the most extreme episode in the GDP growth series.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 11.6 Formal Tests: Applying ADF and KPSS
text:
11.6 Formal Tests: Applying ADF and KPSS
With the visual picture in mind, we now apply both tests formally to all four series.


Show code — ADF and KPSS tests
def run_tests(series, name, adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct"):
    clean = series.dropna()

    adf_res  = adfuller(clean, regression=adf_reg, autolag="AIC")
    kpss_res = kpss(clean, regression=kpss_reg, nlags="auto")

    adf_conc  = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary"
                 if adf_res[1] < 0.05 else
                 "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root")
    kpss_conc = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root"
                 if kpss_res[1] < 0.05 else
                 "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary")

    print(f"\n{'═'*55}")
    print(f"  Series: {name}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")
    print(f"\n  ADF Test  (H\u2080: unit root | spec: '{adf_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {adf_res[0]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {adf_res[1]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (AIC)       : {adf_res[2]:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in adf_res[4].items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {adf_conc}")
    print(f"\n  KPSS Test (H\u2080: stationary | spec: '{kpss_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {kpss_res[0]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {kpss_res[1]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (bandwidth) : {kpss_res[2]:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in kpss_res[3].items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {kpss_conc}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")

run_tests(gdp["Log Real GDP"],      "Log Real GDP (Level)",
          adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct")
run_tests(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"],  "GDP QoQ Growth Rate",
          adf_reg="c",  kpss_reg="c")
run_tests(cpi["Log CPI"],           "Log CPI (Level)",
          adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct")
run_tests(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"],   "Year-on-Year Inflation",
          adf_reg="c",  kpss_reg="c")

The results are consistent with the visual evidence and with the large body of macroeconometrics literature. Log real GDP and log CPI are \(I(1)\): the ADF fails to reject the unit root null and the KPSS rejects stationarity for both level series. After transformation to growth rates and inflation, both tests reverse: the ADF rejects the unit root and the KPSS fails to reject stationarity. Both tests agree in both directions — a clean and reassuring result.
The inflation series deserves a note. The tests suggest stationarity, but the inflation literature is genuinely divided on this question. Inflation appears stationary over long samples but exhibits near-unit-root behavior in particular subperiods — notably the 1970s, when it was both high and highly persistent. The difference between a stationary series with high persistence and a truly unit-root series is statistically difficult to establish with finite samples, and the KPSS result is sensitive to the bandwidth choice. This ambiguity is not a failure of the methods; it is an honest reflection of what the data can and cannot tell us.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.2 The Autocovariance and Autocorrelation Functions
For a weakly stationary process \(\{y_t\}\) with mean \(\mu\), the autocovariance function (ACVF) at lag \(h\) is:
\[\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h}) = \mathbb{E}[(y_t - \mu)(y_{t-h} - \mu)]\]
By weak stationarity, this depends only on \(h\), not on \(t\). Three properties follow immediately: symmetry (\(\gamma(h) = \gamma(-h)\)), maximum at zero (\(|\gamma(h)| \leq \gamma(0) = \sigma^2\), from Cauchy-Schwarz), and positive semidefiniteness (the ACVF matrix is positive semidefinite — not every function can be an autocovariance function).
Normalizing by the variance gives the autocorrelation function (ACF):
\[\rho(h) = \frac{\gamma(h)}{\gamma(0)}, \qquad \rho(0) = 1, \quad |\rho(h)| \leq 1\]
The sample ACF is estimated by:
\[\hat{\rho}(h) = \frac{\hat{\gamma}(h)}{\hat{\gamma}(0)}, \qquad \hat{\gamma}(h) = \frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=h+1}^{T}(y_t - \bar{y})(y_{t-h} - \bar{y})\]
The divisor is \(T\) rather than \(T-h\) to ensure positive semidefiniteness of the sample ACVF matrix. Under the null that the series is white noise, \(\hat{\rho}(h) \overset{a}{\sim} N(0, 1/T)\) for large \(T\), giving the standard 95% confidence bands at \(\pm 1.96/\sqrt{T}\) in ACF plots.
The shape of the ACF immediately reveals temporal structure. A slowly decaying ACF — remaining close to 1.0 across many lags — is the visual fingerprint of a unit root process. A geometrically decaying ACF — dropping steadily toward zero — suggests an autoregressive process. An ACF that cuts off sharply after a small number of lags suggests a moving average process. An ACF indistinguishable from zero at all nonzero lags is the signature of white noise. We will connect these patterns to specific model classes in Chapter 3.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
9.3 The Partial Autocorrelation Function
The ACF measures total correlation between \(y_t\) and \(y_{t-h}\), including indirect effects transmitted through intermediate lags. The partial autocorrelation function (PACF) isolates the direct effect, after removing the linear influence of all intervening observations.
The PACF at lag \(h\), denoted \(\alpha(h)\), is the coefficient on \(y_{t-h}\) in the OLS regression of \(y_t\) on its \(h\) most recent lags:
\[y_t = \phi_{h,1}y_{t-1} + \cdots + \phi_{h,h}y_{t-h} + \varepsilon_t^{(h)}, \qquad \alpha(h) = \phi_{h,h}\]
At lag 1, there is nothing to partial out: \(\alpha(1) = \rho(1)\). At lag 2:
\[\alpha(2) = \frac{\rho(2) - \rho(1)^2}{1 - \rho(1)^2}\]
For general \(h\), the PACF is computed via the Yule-Walker equations — a recursion handled automatically by software.
The ACF and PACF together serve as the primary diagnostic tool for model identification. Their joint behavior reveals the structure of the underlying process and guides the choice of model class and order. Chapter 3 develops these identification patterns in full, once the AR, MA, and ARMA families have been formally introduced. For now, the key skill is computing and reading these functions — the interpretation deepens with each model class we study.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
section: 9.4 Python Example: ACF and PACF for US GDP and CPI
text:
9.4 Python Example: ACF and PACF for US GDP and CPI


Show code — Figure 1.5
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(6, 9))

plot_acf(gdp["Log Real GDP"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[0],
         color=EO_COPPER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_COPPER},
         title="ACF — Log Real GDP (Level)", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(gdp["Log Real GDP"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[1],
          color=EO_COPPER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_COPPER},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Log Real GDP (Level)",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_acf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[2],
         color=EO_SAGE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SAGE},
         title="ACF — GDP Growth Rate", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"].dropna(), lags=20, ax=axes[3],
          color=EO_SAGE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SAGE},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — GDP Growth Rate",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.7)

eo_suptitle(fig, "ACF and PACF: US Real GDP")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 9.1: ACF and PACF for US real GDP. Top two panels: log level. Bottom two panels: QoQ growth rate. The log-level ACF decays extremely slowly — barely moving from 1.0 across twenty lags — the visual fingerprint of an integrated \(I(1)\) process. After log-differencing, the ACF drops sharply and the PACF shows one dominant spike at lag 1. The interpretation of these patterns in terms of specific AR, MA, and ARMA models is developed in Chapter 3.







Show code — Figure 1.6
fig, axes = plt.subplots(4, 1, figsize=(6, 9))

plot_acf(cpi["Log CPI"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[0],
         color=EO_SKYBLUE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SKYBLUE},
         title="ACF — Log CPI (Level)", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(cpi["Log CPI"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[1],
          color=EO_SKYBLUE, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_SKYBLUE},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Log CPI (Level)",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_acf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[2],
         color=EO_LAVENDER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_LAVENDER},
         title="ACF — Year-on-Year Inflation", zero=False, alpha=0.05)

plot_pacf(cpi["Inflation (YoY)"].dropna(), lags=24, ax=axes[3],
          color=EO_LAVENDER, vlines_kwargs={"colors": EO_LAVENDER},
          method="ywm", title="PACF — Year-on-Year Inflation",
          zero=False, alpha=0.05)

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("Lag")
    ax.set_ylabel("Correlation")
    eo_style_ax(ax)
    for line in ax.lines:
        if line.get_linestyle() == "--":
            line.set_color(EO_TERRACOTTA)
            line.set_linewidth(0.7)

eo_suptitle(fig, "ACF and PACF: US CPI")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 9.2: ACF and PACF for US CPI. Top two panels: log level. Bottom two panels: year-on-year inflation. The log-level CPI has a near-unit ACF — clearly nonstationary. The inflation series shows richer autocorrelation structure than GDP growth: the ACF decays slowly and remains significant at many lags, while the PACF drops after a few lags. This persistence reflects, in part, the regime-dependence of inflation dynamics discussed below.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
11.6 Formal Tests Applied


Show code — ADF and KPSS tests
def run_tests(series, name, adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct"):
    clean = series.dropna()
    adf   = adfuller(clean, regression=adf_reg, autolag="AIC")
    kps   = kpss(clean, regression=kpss_reg, nlags="auto")

    adf_conc  = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary"
                 if adf[1] < 0.05 else
                 "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root")
    kpss_conc = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root"
                 if kps[1] < 0.05 else
                 "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary")

    print(f"\n{'═'*55}")
    print(f"  Series: {name}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")
    print(f"\n  ADF Test  (H\u2080: unit root | spec: '{adf_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {adf[0]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {adf[1]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (AIC)       : {adf[2]:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in adf[4].items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {adf_conc}")
    print(f"\n  KPSS Test (H\u2080: stationary | spec: '{kpss_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {kps[0]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {kps[1]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Bandwidth        : {kps[2]:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in kps[3].items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {kpss_conc}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")

The results are clean for all series except inflation. Log real GDP and log CPI are \(I(1)\): the ADF fails to reject the unit root null and the KPSS rejects stationarity for both level series. Their log-differenced counterparts — GDP growth and year-on-year inflation — both show reversal: the ADF rejects the unit root and the KPSS fails to reject stationarity.
The inflation result, however, deserves careful reading and connects directly to the challenge of structural change discussed in Section 3.
Inflation persistence and the Volcker disinflation. The statistical evidence on the stationarity of US inflation is genuinely mixed, and that ambiguity is not a failure of the tests — it is an honest reflection of the history. Before 1979, US inflation was high, volatile, and highly persistent, exhibiting behavior that looks indistinguishable from a unit root process. After 1983, inflation was low, stable, and mean-reverting — unmistakably stationary. The transition between these two regimes was the Volcker disinflation: when Paul Volcker became Federal Reserve Chairman in August 1979, he implemented a sharp and sustained contraction in money growth, deliberately accepting deep recession — unemployment rose above 10% in 1982 — to break the back of entrenched inflation expectations. By 1983, inflation had fallen from above 13% to below 3%.
What changed was not just the level of inflation — it was the structure of the inflation process itself. The behavioral relationships governing wage-setting, price-setting, and expectation formation all shifted when agents recognized that the Fed had genuinely changed its reaction function. This is a textbook illustration of the Lucas critique: the reduced-form persistence of inflation — the estimated AR coefficients — reflected the old policy regime, not deep parameters. When the regime changed, so did the persistence. A forecaster in 1979 who had estimated an inflation model on 1960–1978 data would have found a near-unit-root process. Applying that model to post-1983 data would have grossly overestimated inflation’s tendency to return to high levels.
The practical consequence for our tests: when we pool the full 1960–2024 sample, we are effectively averaging over two quite different regimes. The ADF may marginally reject the unit root because the post-1983 stationary period dominates the long sample; the KPSS may marginally reject stationarity because the pre-1979 unit-root-like behavior pushes the test statistic up. Both reactions are correct responses to the same underlying fact: the process changed. The appropriate response is not to conclude that inflation is or is not stationary, but to recognize that the stationarity assumption itself may be regime-dependent — and to model that regime dependence explicitly when it matters for the analysis at hand.
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.1 The Time Index and Notation
Every time series analysis begins with a convention for labeling observations in time. The time index \(t\) runs over some ordered discrete set — \(\mathcal{T} = \{1, 2, \ldots, T\}\) for a finite sample, or \(\mathcal{T} = \mathbb{Z}\) for the theoretical process underlying the data.
The time index is not just a label. Arithmetic on the index is meaningful: \(y_t - y_{t-1}\) is the change from one period to the next; \(y_{t-12}\) is the value exactly one year ago (for monthly data); \(y_{T+h}\) is the value \(h\) periods beyond the end of the sample. The information set \(\mathcal{F}_t\) denotes everything observable at time \(t\) — the current value \(y_t\), all past values \(y_{t-1}, y_{t-2}, \ldots\), and possibly other relevant variables.
We will use the following notation consistently throughout the course:

\(\mu = \mathbb{E}[y_t]\): the mean of the process
\(\gamma(h) = \text{Cov}(y_t, y_{t-h})\): the autocovariance at lag \(h\)
\(\rho(h) = \text{Corr}(y_t, y_{t-h})\): the autocorrelation at lag \(h\)
\(\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(y_t)\): the variance of the process
\(\varepsilon_t\): an error or innovation term
crumbs: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis

title: 1  Chapter 1: Foundations of Time Series Analysis
text:
6.2 Frequency and Regularity
The frequency of a time series is how often observations are recorded:



Table 6.1: Common time series frequencies in economics and finance





Frequency
Observations per year
Typical use




Annual
1
Long-run growth, cross-country comparisons


Quarterly
4
GDP, national accounts, monetary policy


Monthly
12
CPI, unemployment, industrial production


Weekly
~52
Initial jobless claims, financial flows


Daily
~252 (trading days)
Asset prices, exchange rates


Intraday
Thousands
High-frequency trading, market microstructure






A time series is regular if observations are equally spaced in time. Most macroeconomic data are regular. Some financial series are irregular — individual transaction records arrive whenever a trade is executed — requiring specialized methods we will not cover in this course.

for ax in axes:
    ax.set_xlabel("")

eo_suptitle(fig, "US Real GDP and CPI: Levels and Transformations, 1960–2024")
fig.tight_layout()
plt.show()








Figure 12.1: US real GDP and CPI: log levels and their stationary transformations. The level series (top panels) show persistent upward trends with growing variance — the visual signature of integrated processes. The transformed series (bottom panels) fluctuate around stable means. The COVID-19 contraction and recovery (2020) is the most extreme episode in the GDP growth series; the post-2021 inflation surge is clearly visible in the CPI panel.







Show code — ADF and KPSS tests
def run_tests(series, name, adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct"):
    clean = series.dropna()
    adf   = adfuller(clean, regression=adf_reg, autolag="AIC")
    kps   = kpss(clean, regression=kpss_reg, nlags="auto")

    adf_conc  = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary"
                 if adf[1] < 0.05 else
                 "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root")
    kpss_conc = ("Reject H\u2080 \u2192 Unit root"
                 if kps[1] < 0.05 else
                 "Fail to reject H\u2080 \u2192 Stationary")

    print(f"\n{'═'*55}")
    print(f"  Series: {name}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")
    print(f"\n  ADF Test  (H\u2080: unit root | spec: '{adf_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {adf[0]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {adf[1]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Lags (AIC)       : {adf[2]:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in adf[4].items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {adf_conc}")
    print(f"\n  KPSS Test (H\u2080: stationary | spec: '{kpss_reg}')")
    print(f"  {'─'*50}")
    print(f"  Test statistic   : {kps[0]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  p-value          : {kps[1]:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Bandwidth        : {kps[2]:>9d}")
    for lv, cv in kps[3].items():
        print(f"  Critical val {lv:>4s}: {cv:>9.4f}")
    print(f"  Conclusion       : {kpss_conc}")
    print(f"{'═'*55}")

run_tests(gdp["Log Real GDP"],      "Log Real GDP (Level)",
          adf_reg="ct", kpss_reg="ct")
run_tests(gdp["GDP Growth (QoQ)"],  "GDP QoQ Growth Rate",
          adf_reg="c",  kpss_reg="c")
